items.question: Most valuable thing you took away from exchange?

answers.label: Breaking the waterloo syndrome of all work no fun - plus becoming more spontaneous
How to be more independent, people find a way to make it through tough times
How to get out of the SE rat race and why money doesn't matter as much as you'd think.
I didn't enjoy the European lifestyle as much as I thought I would
Independence and making new friends all by myself in a new city!
Life is short do the new fun things when you’re young while you can
School matters less than the experiences you take away while at school
The ability to make travel plans really quickly and figure out how to use public transportation in Europe lmao
The world is so so big, there are many experiences and opportunities outside of North America and ppl from around the world live such different lives
Theres so much to see and experience in the world

You can do a lot (and have a lot of fun) if you're willing to open your mind and put in the effort to make things happen! Trying different things can surprise you, usually in a good way if you're willing to take a good lesson out of things.
a sense of freedom that stays with me
as much as I'd like to say cultural knowledge or new experiences, it was probably avoiding cs343
perspective and space to reflect on a different lifestyle (madrid life is very slow), spending time/money and what’s important

items.question: Stream 8 vs 8x vs 8y
What language do you use for programming interviews?
Have you ever been late to a co-op interview?
Have you ever missed a co-op interview?
Any fun interview stories?

answers.label:
1. Had an interviewer grill me for 25 minutes on random trivia about programming practices. Dodged a bullet.
2. Was asked about the purpose of a company’s primary product, commented on the company’s architecture. The interviewer said “wow” under his breath and wrote something down. It was the only thing he wrote down, and the only time this ever happened (I’m not cracked) and I got the job :)

A QA position I interviewed for in 1B held all of the interviews on the same Zoom meeting. I joined a few minutes early as I usually do but ended up having to awkwardly sit through the last moments of the previous candidate's interview. The same thing then happened to me at the end of my interview. The interviewer asked me straight up whether I would take the job or not, and I'm pretty sure the fact that I hesitated was the reason why I wasn't ranked.
Absolutely bombed a simple interview because I forgot to study DB stuff, like who just remember what left outer join does for fun. Just show me the ven-diagram picture man
Had an interview scheduled for 12PM, interviewer wanted to reschedule to 4PM, I said okay. By 2PM they sent me an email saying they had found a candidate.

I had to do my final Engineering Manager interview (which was an additional EM round bc the first guy talked too much and we couldn’t get deep enough in my resume) and I was in exchange with my SIGNIFICANT OTHER and we got brunch right before with a friend but I was nervous so I drank 2 mimosas and made them go on a walk around the neighbourhood - best interview of my life actually
I instantly wrote 69 as a test case when asked for larger inputs. I don't think the interviewer noticed and I quickly changed it

I interviewed for a ML position, without having any experience in ML. 10 minutes in the interview and the interviewer asked me to explain a ML concept in simple terms. I debated for a second and then just decided to be honest and told him "I don't know". i told him that I could google it and tell him, but I wouldn't know it otherwise. he started laughing, we had a good laugh about it and then he just ended the interview 15 minutes early.
I once called a company a used car salesman. I also once arrived 30 minutes late, asked for the salary and then said "oh oops sorry i didnt want it anyways".

In-person interview at CECA. I was there 15 minutes early, my interviewer was also there on time. The person at the front desk at CECA somehow didn't call my name until I went and asked about my interview, by which point I was 30 minutes late to the interview.
Interviewed with an upper year SE for his company on waterlooworks at the time

Interviewer asked if I was adventurous, or liked trying new experiences/foods, and I mentioned I was planning to try Takoyaki, aka octopus balls. The interviewer gave me a funny look, and asked "as in... like the part of the octopus?" Found it very funny after I clarified they're balls of batter and octopus meat.
Interviewer left me alone in the interview call for 30 minutes

Interviewer showed up and asked a question I just got asked 30 minutes ago by the previous interviewer (2 interviews back to back for the same company). Was truthful and told them I got asked the question already so they gave me another one. Ended up getting by offer :)
Joined one of my interviews on my alt email as Bob Ross

Multiple occasions where I would take the interview first and launder the questions to friends :P

I once did 60+ interview rounds during a single hiring cycle

I once interviewed at [NYC quant firm] where my interviewer paused the interview halfway through to go and yell at someone over the phone for five minutes (their video was on but thankfully the audio was muted)

Met lots of psycho interviewers at quant firms D:

One startup once asked me an IOI question from the early 2000s :grimace:
My first job interview, I fumbled the reverse palindrome question despite having solved it before.
One company asked me animal care questions

One of the reasons I got my first co-op was because when my interviewer recommended a book, I pulled it off my bookshelf behind me with the bookmark still in it and said I enjoyed it as well. I never finished reading that book.
One time I brought up esports / gaming with my interviewer and we ended up talking about it for the rest of the interview
One time the fire alarm went off in the middle of my interview and I had to evacuate the building. And then I was doing the interview outside while being evacuated from the building.
The leetcode had an O(n) solution and I implemented it in O(n^2), I bombed both questions so badly that the interviewers were speechless and barely tried to say anything.
forgot about an interview to come 15 minutes late and not get the job
interviewer never joined the call, ended up postponing to another day. fire alarm repair ppl enter my room while I'm interviewing and do their thing in the background while I code.
my first ever interview was a technical interview where they had me code in google docs. i did not get the job
while on exchange, I had to do an interview in the middle of the airport and needed to pause halfway because someone was asking for directions in spanish (no hablo espanol lo siento!!).

items.question: What was your co-op role?
How many employees worked at your company?
Which industry was your co-op employer in?
What programming languages did you use at your co-op?
What would you rate your co-op experience?
Favourite overall co-op city?
Anything else co-op related you'd like to add?

answers.label: All my coops were remote, I hated it at first but learned to make the most of it by travelling towards my last coop.
Apply early, apply often, apply to things you don't think you'll get

Ask questions early on because you will look MORE dumb the later you ask speaking from EXPERIENCE

Go for breadth in co-ops and don’t be afraid to take on funky projects because they stand out in a good way !

Prioritize natural friendships in your internships and genuinely make sure you are having fun or else you are doing it wrong 😑
Be ballsy, just straight up reject all the offers you are not interested in. Try to find a place you  feel like home
Cali overtime regulations are very intern-friendly! recommend maxing them out whenever possible
EU/Japan coops were totally worth it (although definitely was not revenue positive)

For work: don't be afraid to ask questions, but try and figure the problem out yourself before going to someone for help, if only so that you can tell them what you've already tried, which will help point both of you in the right direction. Telegraph every design decision you make and document everything you do.

Outside of work: try, as much as you can, to approach everything with excitement and an open mind. Co-op is a wonderful opportunity to meet new people, try new things, and experience new cities - take advantage of that whenever you can!
I wish I applied external more, I wish I fixed my resume earlier, I wish I tried to learn from my coops instead of just doing my hours
Infra is the way. Never give your soul to a company. Make friends, go out, see the world.
Keep applying, even after exams finish
Never give up and stay ambitious

To the people looking for their first co-ops - this is hard! Harder than they make it seem! Don't get too discouraged if you don't make it in the first round, or second, or at all - as long as you don't get complacent, these situations are recoverable.
Try and find work that you like and are good at :D
Try to make local friends especially people who don't work in tech or go to Waterloo
Wish I'd applied more to in-person jobs and/or external stuff but at the end of the day it is what it is
a lot of what you learn is really what you make of the co-op, go off and do ambitious things, ask to be put on projects you find interesting, you get to control your growth
really enjoyed living in different cities and the diversity of experience — would definitely recommend prioritizing trying new things on co-op!

items.question: Advice you'd give your first-year self?

answers.label: Be proactive, and don't fall behind! Early habits matter.
Create side projects early on and explore more by doing more projects and prioritize leetcode. Stay happy and enjoy the process
Do leetcode earlier, put more effort into applying to coops, do coops with the intention to learn

Don't change yourself to conform to a group, no matter how much success "appears" to be guaranteed if you conform, keep your own unique brand and charm and grow it rather than actively diminish it. Prioritize and cut out noise if you have to, and protect your identity.

Don't overthink it. You can worry a bit when things go wrong, but don't worry too much, and for too long. With an open mind, and some creative thought, you'll see possibilities you wouldn't have imagined, although it'll take time to get to that point.
Your life will be so much better if you stop seeking external validation for your problems. Also, start trying to be less defensive now
Just keep going, and make good friends
Life is never that serious, take care of your health and keep loved ones close

Looking for co-ops based on their ratings on WW is the wrong move. Location and pay are the only things you should care about (unless the company sucked during the interviews). Try a bit harder at things that actually matter to you, and less hard at things that you don’t feel great about but are driven by fear to stay ahead on.
Meet more people. Don't be afraid of failure.
Seek advice on building a good resume. Start planning for graduate school early.
Swap to CS/ go on exchange/ talk to more people
Take advantage of every opportunity more
Things do get better with time
Trust the process and keep your head in your own shoes
You have so much going for you, don't ever be afraid to put yourself out there and see what happens
You'll be alright in the end
finding interpsonal relationships is a sampling problem, you should importance sample and your sample count
go out more, uni is a playground for you to live on the extremes before you find out who you really are
good things come to those who persevere. don't give up so easily, even if it feels hopeless
keep a journal so you don't forget how awesome you are
life's about to throw you for a loop, enjoy! remember to sleep eat and exercise.
schools not the most important thing in life. Don't take the people around you for granted, they might not always be there.
take a deep breath, relax, and then get to work
to be more proactive about getting what I want
you can get what you want, but you have to fight for it

item_notes: Interactive route states were rendered to capture co-op #1-#6 and the full academic chart variants.
The published chunks usually expose counts or raw response lists, but they generally do not expose an explicit n value.

items.question: What program are you in?
commentary: Most of our respondents answered that they were in Computer Science this year. However, there were also 12 respondents in CS/BBA and 3 respondents in CFM.

This data might be skewed by, however, one big elephant in the room. Most of our graduates entered school in the famous "225% problem" year, when the entire Math Faculty admitted more than twice their usual admissions target.

items.question: What is your gender identity?
commentary: The majority of respondents identified as men. About a quarter of respondents replied that they were women, however, there were also 2 respondents who identified as being non-binary, 1 respondent who was agender, and 1 respondent who was still figuring things out.

items.question: What are your pronouns?
commentary: The majority of respondents used he/him pronouns, but that does not map one-to-one with gender identities.
We know that gender is a difficult thing, and we wish everyone the best of luck in figuring things out.

items.question: What is your sexuality?
commentary: Most respondents identified as being straight, however, roughly ~8% of respondents identified as being bisexual, which is in line with national statistics, and roughly 4% of respondents identified as being gay/lesbian, which is higher than the national average at 1%.
In total, roughly 15% of respondents were queer, which is a little higher than the national average at 10%.

items.question: What is your birth year?
commentary: While most of the class of 2025 were born in 2002, at least 10% of our respondents either graduated ahead or behind of schedule. However, at the end of the day, life is a winding road, and everyone deserves a pat on the back for finishing a very difficult program. At least there will be no more final exams for this cohort!

items.question: What race/ethnicity best describes you?
commentary: The class of 2025 continues to be as diverse as ever. 81 of our respondents identified the most with being East Asian, 40 identified with being South Asian, 31 with being white, 12 being Southeast Asian, 7 with the Middle East, and 1 respondent identified with being West Asian. 2 of our respondents also identified with being Hispanic/Latino, while 1 respondent identified as being black.

items.question: Do you have a disability?
commentary: 11 of our respondents reported having a disability of some kind, while 4 more were unsure.

items.question: Are you neurodivergent?
commentary: 19 of our respondents identified themselves with being neurodivergent, while 20 of our respondents were unsure.

items.question: What is your religion and/or spiritual affiliation?
commentary: The class of 2025 is very diverse in terms of religious affiliation. While the largest category is atheist/agnostic, which make up around half of all respondents surveyed, there were also 27 Christians, 24 Hindus, 6 Muslims, 3 Buddhists, 2 Sikhs, 1 Jew, and 1 person who identified with being spiritual.

items.question: What is your political alignment?
commentary: 80 respondents identified as being somewhere on the left, 21 respondents identified as being somewhere on the right, while 43 respondents were comfortable in the middle.

items.question: What was your high school admissions average?
commentary: One respondent had an impressive 100 high school average, but perhaps even more impressive, another respondent got in with an 88, which just goes that while the bar (pun intended) to get into CS programs is quite high, grades aren't everything, though it certainly helps.

items.question: What is your MBTI type?
commentary: Unsurprisingly, there are a lot of introverts with 88 responses to that effect, but also a surprising number of extroverts as well with 42 respondents. The most common traits are intuition (104), introversion (88), thinking (80), and judging (80).

items.question: What category of electives for your degree did you enjoy the most?
commentary: People were allowed to select multiple categories. 68 people enjoyed Communications, 70 Humanities, 45 Social Science, 26 Pure Science, 5 Applied Science, 2 Math and 1 ECE. A vast majority of people enjoyed Communications and Humanities electives as a result of their content difference from required courses.

items.question: Which CS course was your favorite?
commentary: We saw a pretty nice distribution of favorites with there being a tie between CS 488 (Introduction to Computer Graphics) and CS 246 / 246E (Object-Oriented Software Development) for most liked.

items.question: Which CS course was your least favorite?
commentary: The most unpopular by a wide margin was CS 245 (Logic & Computation) with 50 students saying they disliked it the most. Other least favorites included CS 350 (Operating Systems) with 26 votes, CS 241 (Foundations of Sequential Programs) with 12, and CS 251 (Computer Organization & Design) with 11.

items.question: Which non-CS course was your favorite?
commentary: There was a wide variety of favorite non-CS courses, as a result of the vast interests of students. A few popular courses included MUSIC 140 (Popular Music & Culture) with 9 votes, MATH 239 (Introduction to Combinatorics) with 7, and MUSIC 246 (Soundtracks: Music in Film) with 6.

items.question: Which non-CS course was your least favorite?
commentary: Just like for the favorites, there is a vast distribution of the least favorite non-CS courses. Common least favorites included STAT 231 (Statistics) with 10 votes, ENGL 109 (Introduction to Academic Writing) with 9, and ECON 101 (Introduction to Microeconomics) with 9 as well.

items.question: What is the hardest course you have taken?
commentary: A large number of students chose MATH 239 (Introduction to Combinatorics) and CS 350 (Operating Systems) as their hardest course, with 27 and 21 votes respectively.

items.question: What is the easiest course you have taken?
commentary: A great majority of students (50) chose CLAS 104 (Classical Mythology) as their easiest class. ECON 101 and CLAS 202 come in a not so close second and third, with 7 and 6 votes respectively.

items.question: Which course do you regret taking the most?
commentary: There is a vast amount of courses students might regret taking, but the most regretful courses showed to be CS 486 (Introduction to Artificial Intelligence) with 10 votes, and CS 348 (Introduction to Database Management) with 8 votes.

items.question: Which course did you find the most useful?
commentary: A good amount of people seemed to agree that CS 341 (Algorithms) and CS 246 (Object Oriented Software Development) are the most useful courses, with 37 and 30 votes respectively.

items.question: Which course did you find the least useful?
commentary: On the other hand, CS 245 (Logic & Computation) , CS 251 (Computer Organization & Design) , and CLAS 104 (Classical Mythology) seemed to be the most useless courses.

items.question: Did you take any enriched courses?
commentary: Some courses do have an enriched variant, where you can learn about the course material more in depth. 47 students chose to take advantage of these courses.

items.question: If you took an enriched course, please indicate how much you enjoyed it.
commentary: It seems as though many students who took enriched courses enjoyed them, with only 11/45 students choosing a score less than 4.

items.question: Did you transfer into your current program?
commentary: Most people who graduated initially started in their program, but 30 transferred in.

items.question: What program did you transfer from?
commentary: Most people seemed to have transferred from MATH, with others transferring from other programs including the Double Degree programs and Engineering. 1 student also transferred from another school.

items.question: In which term did you transfer into your program?
commentary: Most students transferred early on in their schooling, while a few transferred in 3rd year.

items.question: What were your reason(s) behind transferring?
commentary: People had a few different reasons for wanting to transfer into CS, with the most popular reasons being because they wanted CS course access, or they didn't like their old major.

items.question: Who is your favorite professor at UW?
commentary: We have many professors in the CS department, but the top 3 seemed to be Jeff Avery, Brad Lushman, and Jeff Orchard.

items.question: Which study term did you think was the hardest?
commentary: A great majority of students found 2B to be the hardest, with a lot of the harder required 2nd year courses appearing then.

items.question: Which study term did you think was the easiest?
commentary: Most people found 4th year terms to be the easiest, since they tend to not have as many required courses. A close 3rd was 3B.

items.question: What was your average for each term?
What was your cumulative average?
commentary: Our graduates obtained very respectable cumulative averages this term! CS is a fairly difficult program, so we're proud of everyone who completes this program regardless of their grade.

items.question: What was your faculty average?
commentary: Overall, we observed a higher mean faculty average but a lower median compared to the cumulative average.

items.question: Which option(s) did you complete?
commentary: We saw a handful of students complete some options for, gaining valuable experience not directly part of the major. Options are a great way to broaden your qualifications.

items.question: Which minor(s) did you complete?
commentary: Minors are also a great way to customize and add to your degree and can be a valuable addition to your portfolio and resume.

items.question: Which specialization(s) did you complete?
commentary: If you are particularly passionate about a specific topic, specializations can be a great way to focus your studies and gain depth of experience in a specific field.

items.question: How many times did you overload a term?
commentary: An overloaded term can be a way to get ahead in your degree, but it can also be quite challenging. 70 students chose not to take an overloaded term, but we also saw students take up to 8 overloaded terms.

items.question: How many courses have you failed?
commentary: Most of the class graduated without failing a class, but plenty did and still graduated! So, even if you fail one or a few of your courses, it's not the end of the world.

items.question: Prior coding experience
commentary: While prior coding experience is an asset, it is not required for admission into CS at Waterloo. Though, exposure to a variety of coding opportunities may help in determining whether or not computer science is something you want to pursue in university.
A majority of students learned CS through courses in high school and on their own time, with a decent amount having little to no prior programming experience.

items.question: How many programming languages do you know?
commentary: Most respondents claim to know around 7-10 programming languages. This is to be expected, considering the variety of languages that students are exposed to throughout their courses, side-projects, co-ops, hackathons, and other coding opportunities.

items.question: What is your favourite programming language?
commentary: There is an evident preference towards Python and C++, likely due to both languages being featured predominantly throughout the CS curriculum and within the industry. Shoutout to the singular Racket enjoyer!

items.question: What is your favourite editor/IDE?
commentary: Unsurprisingly, VS Code is the predominant code editor among the class of 2025. It is a versatile and lightweight code editor with a plethora of handy open-source extensions and customization features. It was with great disappointment to see the lack of Microsoft Word or Windows Movies Maker users in the survey data.

items.question: What operating systems do you use?
commentary: There is a fairly even split between Windows and macOS users, with a slight preference towards macOS among respondents. This is likely due to Macbooks–especially the M-series-being well known for their portability and overall strong performance. macOS also comes with the added benefit of being UNIX-based like Linux, which comes with various tools and features that cater to developers.

items.question: What is your Leetcode Proficiency?
commentary: Leetcode is an online coding platform where you can solve problems relating to data structures and algorithms as preparation for technical interviews. The majority of respondents claim to have around moderate levels of proficiency with Leetcode.

items.question: How many side projects do you have?
commentary: Side projects are great ways to explore and learn new technologies, build on your interests, and enhance your resume for potential internships.
On average, students have built around 4 side projects throughout their studies in CS. Though, the majority of students have done three or fewer side projects.

items.question: How many hackathons have you attended as a hacker?
commentary: Hackathons are an amazing way to meet all sorts of people, learn new technologies, win cool swag, and build whatever your heart desires. There are many hackathons held by clubs and committees in Waterloo and around the world throughout the year, where students of all skill levels are welcome to participate.
Roughly 76% of students have had the opportunity of at least one hackathon throughout their undergraduate career, with the majority only attending one.

items.question: Are you in a co-op program?
commentary: Out of the 166 responses we received, 160 of them indicated that they were in coop, while 5 said they were not. Co-op is overall a popular choice among Waterloo students, and opens the door to many opportunities.

items.question: What was your favorite co-op location?
commentary: Toronto and New York are the top favourite 2 destinations for co-op students. They are both large cities with many things to do and many large companies, so it comes as no surprise that many students prefer these places

items.question: Have you ever had a co-op term without a placement?
commentary: Out of the responses we received, 128 (87.7%) people had placements every term. However, don't despair if you don't get a placement, as 18 (12.3%) respondants had a work term without a placement or participated in WE Accelerate.
Finding a co-op placement especially in this job market can be fairly difficult. Don't give up!

items.question: What were the coolest non-salary perks that you received from a company during co-op?
commentary: Many co-ops offered free or discounted food or drinks, and it was a hit among the co-op students. Co-ops also privded other cool perks, such as free techhnology or transportation

items.question: How frequently did you cook for yourself?
commentary: Most people had home-cooked meals somewhere between daily and weekly, lessening in frequency as the interval between home-cooked meals increased. In other words, most of the graduates are functional adults who know how to cook.

items.question: How physically active were you?
commentary: Staying active is important for your health, and Waterloo CS students are definitely not beating the sweaty stereotypes this year with how often they went to the gym. Most of the students went to the gym 3-6 days of the week with only a handful going 0-2 times a week or 7 days a week.

items.question: What extracurricular activities were you a part of?
commentary: Although CS students face tough co-op searches and brutal course loads, many still find time to participate in a variety of ECs. Many stay fit with intramurals or varsity sports, while others join more unique clubs like Cheese Club, Humans vs Zombies or Poker Club. Some notable answers include: Orientation volunteering, Board Games Club Underwater Hockey Jam Network Socratica UW Esports.

items.question: What non-CS hobbies do you have?
commentary: Waterloo students have a wide range of hobbies, but badminton, gaming, going to the gym, and reading are definitely at the top! These activities offer a perfect escape, making the grind of university life just a bit more enjoyable.

items.question: Did you have enough time to pursue those hobbies during study terms?
commentary: Most students at least somewhat felt they had enough time to pursue their hobbies during study terms, though a smaller group still said no outright. Hobbies are a good way to de-stress, maintain balance, and make university life feel more sustainable during heavier academic stretches.

items.question: Did you have enough time to pursue those hobbies during work terms?
commentary: Most students said they had enough time to pursue their hobbies during work terms, with a smaller group saying only somewhat and very few saying no. Compared with study terms, work terms appear to leave more room for routines, recovery, and interests outside of school.

items.question: Were you a part of any clubs or community groups purely for fun? If so, which ones?
commentary: Never never forget to have fun!

items.question: What was your go-to exam fuel? (e.g. coffee, energy drinks, bubble tea, etc.)?
commentary: The majority answered coffee or other. Energy drinks, sleep/none, food, bubble tea, water, and normal tea were balanced relatively others, some of the (slightly concerning) responses include: Beef patty Almonds Anxiety Adderall Stress

items.question: How many hours of sleep did you get per night on average during study terms?
commentary: Once again, the graduates who’ve participated in this survey have beaten the allegations. The vast majority of people in this survey have typical, healthy sleep schedules with a reasonable 7-10 hours of sleep per night.

items.question: How many hours of sleep did you get per night on average during work terms?
commentary: Beyond their 9-5s, the participants in this survey actually managed to get more sleep on average than during their work terms. If more graduates in high finance or quant were to participate in this survey, the results might be different; however, accounting for the reasonable hours of most tech jobs, this checks out.

items.question: What time did you usually go to sleep?
commentary: Clear normal distribution centred around 12-2 AM. Depending on one’s schedule, sleep time can vary quite a bit. If you are blessed enough not to have any morning lectures/tutorials, you may be able to go to bed at 4 and still get a reasonable amount of sleep.

items.question: How often did you pull all-nighters?
commentary: Most Waterloo graduates this year seem to have good time management skills, as the majority of people never, or only occasionally, pull all-nighters. Sending strength to those in the bottom two categories o7.

items.question: How often did you sleep in?
commentary: Most graduates repaid their sleep debt weekly, likely on the weekends. Some were more taxed with debt, only being able to sleep without a care occasionally, whereas the 22 that got to sleep in daily are the envy of everyone else.

items.question: How often did you attend parties?
commentary: I can safely say that most of what you’ve heard of Waterloo is true; not much happens here. Most of the graduates never partied or only did so occasionally. For the party animals, how do you even find all those parties? There can’t be that many people raving on a weekend in the middle of November or a random Friday in March.

items.question: How often did you hang out with friends during study terms?
commentary: Students naturally spend more time with their friends during study terms due to proximity. Most students either have a consistent social life or a balance between hangouts and solitude.

items.question: How often did you hang out with friends during work terms?
commentary: Surprisingly, the daily hangout group increased in size, possibly because co-op students may socialize with other interns at their placements. Additionally, students on their work terms generally have more free time than during study terms. On the other hand, the number of students with infrequent hangouts also increased, taking from the weekly batch in the process.

items.question: How often did you go on vacations during university?
commentary: The majority of those surveyed went on vacation every few months, likely at the end of their terms. The group that never travelled was also prevalent in this question, with those that travelled more frequently making up a negligible portion of the population.

items.question: How many hours did you spend doomscrolling per week on average during university?
commentary: Short-form content consumption is prevalent among our student body. The data shows an even spread through all intervals, suggesting that Waterloo students actually have more time on their hands than expected, with the only limiting factor being self-control.

items.question: What was your favourite way to relax after a long day of classes or work?
What drugs have you tried?
commentary: For most, college is the first time many students get to experiment with substances. Whether it's peer pressure, stress or curiosity, the most common substance experimented with, during university, is Cannabis, Alcohol and Caffeine, with Alcohol and Caffeine having been explored before university. With that being said, one must be cautious when experimenting with substances, ensuring that they are in a safe environment when trying out unfamiliar substances.

items.question: Have you ever had sexual intercourse?
commentary: Besides substances, for most, college is also the first time many students get to experiment with their sexuality. While this newfound freedom, free from parental control, may feel empowering, it is important to recognize that one does not rush into figuring it all out. Taking time is natural and a crucial part of the journey.

items.question: If so, in which term did you first have sexual intercourse?
commentary: Whether it’s before university begins or during the third year, losing one’s virginity is a deeply personal journey. Not everyone has to follow the same timeline or feel pressured to do so. What matters most is ensuring that one takes things at their own pace and are not being rushed.

items.question: How many relationships have you been in during university?
commentary: Majority of students have had at least one relationship in university, with 34% respondents never having been in a relationship during university.

items.question: Are you currently in a relationship?
commentary: 54% of the respondents are currently not in a relationship while 42% do have a partner.

items.question: What portion of your university experience was spent in a relationship?
commentary: As seen earlier, nearly 36% of students have not been in a relationship during their time at the university. Of the students that did have a relationship, 26% were in it for roughly 30% of their time at Waterloo. The remaining students were in a relationship for more than 1/3rd of their university experience.

items.question: Have you ever been caught up in a cheating situation
commentary: While the majority of respondents have not cheated during a relationship, there is a minor group of students that have been cheated on, cheated or caught in the middle of the mess.

data.category: Yes, I've been an innocent third-party caught up in the mess

items.question: What is your Rice Purity score?
commentary: The Rice Purity test is a 100-question survey designed to measure a person’s level of innocence based on the experiences they have had during their time at university. This survey covers topics ranging from relationships to substance and more. A higher score indicates having more ‘purity’. Nearly 25% of the respondents have a Rice Purity score between 90 and 100 and 50% of the respondents have a rice purity score between 60 – 100.

items.question: What is your Waterloo Purity Test score?
commentary: The Waterloo Purity Test is also a 100-question survey designed to track one’s experiences during their time at university. This survey covers topics ranging from relationships to substance and more. Nearly 25% of the respondents have a Waterloo Purity Score between 80 to 100 and 50% of the respondents have a Waterloo Purity Score between 70 and 100.

items.question: Do you have any advice for people looking for a relationship?
commentary: Most of the advice shared highlighted the importance of self-confidence and the belief that relationships will naturally develop over time. Rather than forcing a connection, the key is to focus on being comfortable with yourself and letting things unfold when the right person comes along.

data: Be a good person and be honest about yourself
Don't look for it. The more you look, the more you won't find it.

Besides the traditional wisdom of just putting yourself out there, my best advice is to know what you need, what you're looking for, and to not gaslight yourself into believing otherwise. To some degree, this does require a bit of experimentation, but to a larger degree, this means being able to say no to people, to break it off when you know it's not right for you. This can't save you from heartbreak, but will help you find what you want in the end. The trouble is figuring out what you need and not just what you want.
Be earnest and kind. and love will find you
Try to have the best possible conversation you can with a potential partner
Dont use dating apps for love, ur not finding the one there
Know what you want during a relationship, don't get into a relationship just because you have a crush with someone.
Work on yourself and have good friends to support you
Learn respect for yourself and for others
Focus on social connection and self-confidence. People are not objects or trophies.
Be happy with your own life first
Be open and honest about your feelings and try to make genuine connections with people, don't just look for quick hookups.

Be yourself, not because that's what'll attract people but because that's what'll make a lasting relationship. At the end of the day, if someone loves an image you're presenting, it won't be sustainable or satisfying. There's someone for everyone and it's important to find the right match for your true self

items.question: Do you have a full-time position lined up?
commentary: 8 out of every 11 respondents have a full-time position lined up after completing their degree. The remaining 45 respondents that have not secured a full-time role yet might either be exploring other roles that better suit their interests or may have plans to pursue their post-graduate education, as seen later on.

items.question: What company is this job at?
commentary: With 32 respondents having secured a full-time opportunity at Amazon (including Amazon Robotics & AWS), Meta, Bloomberg and Data Bricks, these companies are the top 4 popular destinations where respondents chose to further their career pursuits.

items.question: What position is this job?
commentary: Nearly 75% of all respondents who have secured a full-time role, have committed to a position in Software Development & Engineering. The next most popular job titles were Full Stack Developer and Backend engineer. Some interesting roles that graduates are planning to take on are that of a power analytics intern, Quant developer and R&D engineer.

items.question: What location is this job at?
commentary: With California being the home to Silicon Valley, California remains a popular location among the respondents to pursue their career, while Ontario takes the lead in Canada. Washington, British Columbia and New York are some of the other popular locations too, along with remote offers. One responder shares that their role invites them to the Netherlands.

items.question: What is the total estimated value of your first year compensation package in CAD? (including salary, bonus, first-year stock, etc.)
commentary: From the first-year compensation packages generated, we can see that of the 30 respondents that hold full time offers in Canada, majority have first year compensations between 100000 – 200000 CAD. This trend is also evident amongst those that received remote offers. On the other hand, respondents with offers from outside Canada typically have the majority of full-time compensations between 200000 – 300000 CAD.

items.question: Is this a return offer from a prior co-op?
commentary: Co-op experience not only offers students a chance to explore what working in their field of interest is like but also network with their colleagues and demonstrate their professional acumen. With nearly 7 out of every 10 offers being return offers, coop proves to be a valuable tool in furthering one's professional interests.

items.question: If so, which co-op term was it from?
commentary: Of the 5-6 coop terms taken, the majority of respondents receiving a return offer got one from their final few coops. This could be because by the 5th or 6th coop, respondents not only identify their fields of interest, but also develop their hard & soft skills.

items.question: Were you referred (for the full-time position or for the co-op position if this is a return offer)?
commentary: While coop can help build one's professional network, one does not necessarily need a referral to secure a full-time position. As suggested by the data collected, nearly 85% of the respondents that secured a full-time position did so without a referral.

items.question: What career path are you pursuing post-grad?
commentary: With nearly 75% of the class of 2025 intending to pursue a career path in this field, Software Development and Engineering (including backend software) is one of the most sought after fields post-grad. Following this, the second most popular field for post-graduate career pursuits is Machine Learning. Some unique fields respondents intend to pursue include Quant Dev, Production Engineering, Security and DevOps.

items.question: Are you planning on pursuing post graduate education?
commentary: Everyone's career follows a unique path. For 14% of the respondents, this means pursuing a postgraduate degree, while for 50% of the respondents this is not the case.

items.question: If so, what are you planning to pursue?
commentary: Of those pursuing post-graduate education, 3 have committed to completing a combined Masters + PhD program. Other popular academic pursuits include a Master's in Mathematics, Master's in computer science, MBA, and even Law!

items.question: Are you planning to study abroad?
commentary: While the majority of the respondents do not intend to study abroad after the completion of their degree, nearly 13% look forward to exploring their post-graduate studies abroad, popular destinations for which are discussed below.

items.question: If studying abroad, where are you planning to move?
commentary: Realistically, committing to studying abroad hugely depends on which universities one gets accepted to. While the majority of respondents acknowledge this and attribute the destination of their post-graduate pursuits on their acceptances, a popular choice to pursue masters is the United States.

items.question: How would you rate your mental health over your entire undergraduate career?
commentary: Mental health ratings varied across the scale. Most students rated their mental health as a 3 or 4 out of 5. While 13 students rated their experience as a 5, showing resilience and well-being, others faced more significant challenges, as indicated by the smaller group of students rating it a 1 or 2. Overall, it's a reminder that mental health can fluctuate, and supporting one another through the highs and lows is important.

items.question: What was the lowest point in your university career?
commentary: As opposed to 2024, when a lot of students answered their 1A term, for the Class of 2025, the lowest point in their university journey seems to be pretty evenly split, with more focus on the 2nd, 3rd, and 4th years study terms. A lot of stress seems to be coming from the co-op job hunt, that Waterloo grind both makes us and breaks us… Other significant low points included personal struggles like romantic or friendship breakups, COVID-19, academic stress, and living arrangements.

items.question: What mental health conditions have you experienced?
commentary: About half of the respondents from the class of 2025 reported experiencing some sort of mental health condition. Including about 44% experiencing anxiety, and 38% experiencing depression. Other conditions include eating disorders, PTSD, OCD, personality disorders, and a few others.

items.question: Have you ever experienced burnout?
commentary: Burnout remains a significant concern, with more than 78% of respondents indicating they experienced it at some point during their undergraduate career. The demanding workload and fast-paced environment can take a toll, leaving students feeling overwhelmed or exhausted. Remember that these feelings are common and that taking time for rest and self-care is crucial in maintaining mental and emotional well-being.

items.question: Have you ever experienced suicidal ideation?
Have you ever been to therapy?
commentary: 25.7% of respondents have attended therapy, while 17.4% have considered it but not gone. While therapy does not end up helping everyone, there is no shame in reaching out and getting support.

items.question: Have you ever been to counselling?
commentary: 25.2% of respondents have attended counselling, while 9.1% considered it but did not go. Like with therapy, it's not for everyone, and there are many ways to get help and handle mental health issues, but many people end up finding it helpful in one way or another.

items.question: Have you ever used UW Health Services for your mental health?
commentary: The majority of the graduating class of 2025 (74.1%) have not used UW Health Services for mental health support, but still, for many, it's a valuable and easily accessible resource.

items.question: If so, how was your experience with UW Health Services?
commentary: A majority of students give their experience with UW Health Services a 4 out of 5, relatively positive; however, the rest give a pretty evenly spread out rating ranging from a 1 to a 5.

items.question: How much of an effect has imposter syndrome played in your university life?
commentary: Imposter syndrome significantly impacted many students in the graduating class of 2025. Most responses fell in the upper range, indicating higher impact, with 4 being the most common rating. It's important to remember that feeling this way is okay and a normal part of growth and learning.

items.question: What has been your experience with imposter syndrome?
commentary: Imposter syndrome seems to be most commonly experienced when it comes to co-op. It is important that unless you literally played unfairly, then if you got something, you must deserve it - luck is a part of strength. Furthermore, remember that everyone has their own paths, and you don't have to compare yourself with others.

items.question: Do you have any general comments about mental health in university and/or at UW?

data: Waterloo's reputation is a bit exaggerated, particularly online. I think similar issues would persist regardless of the university.
Not much to say, other than please make things easier for people? never had any experience with the system here

The difficulty of UW's STEM programs plus its co-op system with diverging sequences is not a good combination for student mental health. But Campus Wellness has actually been very helpful to me, and there are clubs that can give you a more reliable sense of community.
Reach out to people, talk to others. We all help each other to get through this.
You need to make an effort to make a community here or else you will go insane

UW (especially CS/ENG) will destroy you if you let it. Even if you succeed in class and get a sick co-op, you will end up feeling completely empty and unfulfilled if you let the toxic rat race culture define your time here. Most of getting good jobs is luck and being a nice person to the right people, but many here will try to imply that getting a good job is a reflection of your personal worth.

Co-op/Job wise, I've seen UW and the toxic environment affect people's mental, and I get where it's coming from, but the university provides the opportunities and if anything the people are what make it toxic. I don't know if anything can be done to change/fix it, kind of just seems like it's bound to happen with a cohort of super smart people vying for the same jobs. Academics wise, courses can be hard but doable, just need a good support system I guess?

items.question: What do you do to help manage your mental health challenges?
commentary: The graduating class of 2025 utilizes a wide range of coping strategies to manage mental health challenges. The most popular strategies include getting more sleep, talking it out, or other self-care activities such as journaling, working out, etc. Overall, the class demonstrates a strong sense of resilience and the importance of maintaining a good support system and healthy habits.

So... in first year, I was a starry-eyed kid, not knowing how to cook, not knowing how to do anything. Back in MKV, I just learned how to boil dumplings the week before, and started on grinding for hackathons. On one fateful day, after grinding for a hackathon with a buddy, I wanted some freshly baked frozen pizza. I unwrapped the pizza, grabbed my roommate's tray and started cooking. After the pizza started smelling nice and ready, I started looking at the oven. And lo and behold, the pan started to warp a bit. I thought to myself, that looks weird, isn't it? I opened the door and saw the pan melt down towards the bottom of the oven, forming a puddle. This puddle was no ordinary puddle, as it caught on fire and caught me in a whirlwind of panic. I call my hackathon buddy up and show him the mess, only to face endless laughter.

After sending my roommate down for a fire extinguisher, I hear the fire alarm going off, sending everyone outside as the firemen came, on this particular cold September night. Needless to say, as everyone wondered who burned their oven, I too joined in on the speculation.

items.question: What's the best lesson you learned at UW?

data: Be yourself and take up space, the world needs more people like you
Never give up. Just because you can't do it today doesn't mean it's you can't ever do it.
Apply everywhere
Be scared of geese
you can do whatever you want if you care enough
Everything can be learned
It's not that deep
Proper schedule is key.
dont do group projects with random people
put trust in people
There's always a bigger fish.
people are weird bro
People are weird
I have learned what
nothing lasts forever so enjoy it while you can :)
That learning how you learn, work, and think is more valuable than any single course.
Learning how to learn, and being someone who knows how to start improving themselves.
start early
just be a good person and seek out things you like
Even though things seem bad now, in the end, it all works out! One minor setback doesn't define your future
Life ends up sorting itself out, don't stress too much
You can just walk up and talk to people
Don't take hard classes alone, ever. If you have to, make sure you make some friends there fast.
Organize yourself; slow and steady work over time beats spontaneous hard work followed by a period of lull.
Never trust Portal
there's more to life than cracked jobs
there will always be someone naturally smarter and more gifted than you. god plays favorites but that doesn't mean you're fucked. just put in the effort, get lucky, and maximize your chances
Self-reliance is a rare and wonderful thing
courage is in shorter supply than intelligence
be yourself, cliches and common advice exist for a reason (learn from them!)
We are all very different, and being myself is ok.
Your worth isn't determined by the people around you but by the people you surround yourself with.
This is a factory that produces engineers, and I'm just another artifact. Grow some balls and move on...gotta keep going
A small close-knit group is all you need to be happy
If you want something done right, do it yourself. Don't trust others completely - they'll let you down eventually.
touch grass
The answer is in the textbook, look for it
People are better than you, accept it, live with it, or cope 24/7
How to be strong and independent and get through the harder times in life. Although this degree hurt me in a lot of ways it also showed me how strong I am and how I can persevere.
The best opportunities don't fall from the sky, you need to search for them and fight for them.

all of 4B

items.question: When was your absolute hardest time at UW? (We won't make you relive it for too long.)

data: Loneliness and anxiety from the imposter syndrome. It was a long journey to get over
Realizing I was probably going to fail my MATH 239 midterm while writing it. (I got a 50 though big W)
Imposter Syndrome
2B assignments
Studying 3 weeks of never-seen-before content in 2 days for STAT 231
Situationship
2nd year
Overloading + coop search in 1B.
The days of trying to transfer to CS from MATH.
2B. Tough 6 course loads with 5 major CS/Math courses plus coop job search, and that got me a transfer from math to CS. Hard times create strong ppl.
school in the summer

Getting matched with a co-op job far away in Toronto and not having housing arranged, and having to find someone to take over my Campus Housing contract on short notice - I could only blame myself for ranking the job when I didn't plan that far ahead, but fortunately things worked out.
being snaked by highschool friends when i was getting help for depression, dealing with an abusive relationship and having to do 3rd year at the same time
When I found out a girl was slandering me to get my attention
2B taking 4 required CS courses, one of them being Math239, COVID restrictions getting half-lifted so switching to in-person class
Getting rejected from MIT was rough, but worry not, I shall apply again after a masters!
2B term - only God knows how I passed MATH 239
cs 451 project p2

Going through heartbreak to be honest. That's something that doesn't necessarily get easier without a lot of work on oneself. You'd need to pick up the pieces of yourself, and try to understand where you've gone wrong and how you can be a better person independent of trying to define yourself in terms of someone else.
CS 240 and 341 - hated those courses
Crying in my bed during online school because I was doing math 10 hours a day and I felt totally alone
Online during the pandemic in 2A, barely keeping up with classes and having to look for a co-op
My 2B term when classes switched back from being online to being fully in person halfway through the term. I had gotten so lazy I nearly failed the whole term.
First in-person midterm. Math 239.
MATH 138
2B/3A terms so many all nighters
doing cs 241 assignments back to back in mc lab
Job searching in continuous round in first year.
didnt you ask this on the last page

losing 5 job interviews because i sucked? i had a pretty good experience, nothing too bad.
doing CS341 and CS350 alone
Studying first-year courses remotely during the pandemic on the other side of the Earth, without cheating.
Almost failing courses and being in a situationship
This term. Took too harsh of a course load
Getting Policy 71'd for helping someone out ;-;
Every group project ever.
3a exams
During my 2B term, loneliest I've ever felt in my life
Wenhu Chen CS486

It was during 2A/2B I felt so isolated and the workload was insane. I didn't really know how to handle the work and being alone all the time and I got extremely depressed, nothing mattered to me anymore except passing school which I knew I had to get through because tutition was so expensive. I would pull multiple all nighters every week and soon I couldn't recognize myself in the mirror anymore which led to further body image struggles. I still suffer with this but therapy has helped me a lot.
Right before the STAT 231 final, when I WDed the course. I redid it the next term and fared much better.
3A term taking super hard classes and working part time
attacked by goose
grinding out the cs246e project during exam season
The time from 1B to 2A when I slacked off after completing 1A, and cannot recover from demotivated to work.
444 during midterms
2A, having to do 4 core courses during COVID from my room including MATH239 and CS245. unbelievably painful having 3 assignments due weekly consistently
Studying for exams for the first 4 hours of the day, having a 1 hour break to go to counselling and cope w/ breakup, then 4+ more hours of study.
Trying to open my assignment grades in 1A and 1B with shaky hands.
Not being able to live life until I got the CS145 final grade back
Algorithms (CS341) and OS (CS350) in one term
Doing my first in person study term - felt uber stupid and worse than every single person in my classes
1A
coop search + getting rejected from a US research internship + dealing with a friend's breakup + full courseload with one of them being CS 480 + seasonal depression all at once
4A overly hard course
2B semester. Too much work, too many drugs, too few friends
4A term. If I cannot pass CS350 I would be out of CS Program for real (for the record I have stacked up 2 WDs but no fails with CS350 before).
2B or not to be. And 2A.
Weekly assignments and non stop grind
4A when all hell broke lose and I couldnt focus on anything
When it seemed like every one I knew popped off and had a better job than me.
Stat 231
2A. My girlfriend cheated on me. :(
Had to postpone my birthday because I had too many assignments due all at the same time.

items.question: Any fun interview stories?

responses: I slept through my first interview ever
I waited in front of a professor's door for 2 hours so I can chat with him and got a job offer from it
Asked why my marks were so low
An interviewer asked me “How would you test a watermelon?” Another interviewer asked me “Are you smart?”
The power to CMH went out during one of my interviews. Didn't get the job :(

Interviewed for a small tech company in SF. I was on a call with the CEO of the company, and it wasn't through WaterlooWorks so I let him know that the deadline was very soon and if he could get back to me within the next week that would be great. I get an email a couple days later telling me they're sorry, they've decided to go in a different direction on the internship, but could I please send them my address because they have some things they want to send me. I then get a package with like $300 worth of computer parts and some branded merch
Taught my interviewer python (this was a python coding interview)
One of my interviews had his daughter in the background showcasing and taking photos of her feet.
I've had interviewers not show up, or show up late and joined the interview from their car.
As an EE with mostly software experience - interviewing for an EE role, he told me to never switch to EE jobs (as in my EE skills are shit)
Rushed home in a cab drunk a 12am because I misremembered when I scheduled my interview
During an interview, the interviewer flat out said that there might not even be any funding for the job and he might just hire a high schooler to do it for free instead.
interviewer left during the interview lol
Once submitted code so bad it broke the autograder
I was late to my first co-op interview because of Windows update.
Flew to Waterloo for an in person interview without telling my employer at the time
Interviewer was walking down the stairs during one of my interviews. Proceeded to trip over his cat and do the rest of the interview on the floor.
Huawei interviewer took the interview on his phone in potrait. Appeared to be disinterested. Grilled me on irrelevant questions to the job posting.
0 technical questions, talked about boats for 30 minutes. Got the job.
Being the only interviewee to solve the coding challenge only to be told I was too young so they weren't going to rank me.
Critiquing my boyfriends painting behind me together with the interviewer
Didn't realize an interview was in-person until 1 hour before
My Japanese roommate who didn't speak english interrupted a remote interview to bring me a cream puff she made me <3
I did an interview on the train
my interviewer was vaping. I don't think he even realized - it was probably muscle memory for him. I saw him realize as he was about to hit it, and he did then tried to play it off cool and hide it
Fluffed up my resume too much, interviewer figured it out, just started talking about life instead

items.question: Do you have any coop-specific advice to share? Would you do anything differently?

responses: Always apply big, apply for the dream jobs whether through WW or externally, you never know when you'll hit.
No one else actually knows what they’re doing either
Start early and find a niche that you are into early. Don't just follow the crowd.
Use one of my co-ops to do something different than embedded software
Don't stress too much about early co-ops, upper-year glowup is real. If you focus your interests/projects in one area, you'll have an easier time standing out.
Do not return to the same employer unless the role provides significant learning experiences.
Start doing projects/learning actual software engineering (not just coding) early.
Don't stress too much about it, and take the opportunity to apply for weird shit and things you're super unqualified for just for the fun of it and the experience.
Pick a niche that you can beat 99.9% of everyone else in
I would apply to more external jobs and try to travel more
Coop is what you make it out to be. Even if you arent satisfied with your role, reach out, and you'd be surprised what you can dom

If you want to do hardware, don't join the crowds and become a code monkey like everyone else in first year. Join a design team and get experience in the field of your choice. The barrier to entry for good companies is lower than one might think.

Confidence is key, don't be afraid to ask for help and make sure you convince the interviewers that you're the best person for the job As for doing stuff differently, not really. Maybe if I grinded I could've gotten some US internships but I'm kind of a lazy bum so yea
Maybe try a few new places.
Worry less about not doing side projects/co-ops in my free time - I used to feel really guilty. Instead open source contribution and club related achievements go a lot further.

Being likable is just as important as being talented or hardworking. If you just go into work, head down, grinding, you're not gonna get as far as the person who others want to help and want to see succeed.
Grind leetcode ASAP. Building cool stuff like personal projects/design teams stand out. Grades matter! Good grades are a good quantifiable signal of your technical skills and hard work.
Find a field to specialize in. Some fields are easier to get into than others, but there are challenging problems in every field, so expertise is always in demand.
Don’t use WW. Also, cold outreach to startups is the easiest way to find the first few internships
Specialize in a field, it pays dividends to have domain expertise
Apply to everything. Like seriously, even if you aren't a good fit for what they interviewed you for, if you did a good job they may redirect you to a different team.
Always apply externally, keep looking for a better job, money is king.
My biggest regret is not reneging and going for a better company; I was scared of the consequences.
Join design teams. I regret not doing the same seeing where it took people for co-ops and full time positions
Apply to as many jobs as possible regardless of whether you actually meet the requirements (you're going to learn them on the job anyway).
If I did it again I would apply more externally instead of relying on WW
Your worth as a human isn't based on how much money you can make! Big tech shouldn't be your goal just because it's some people's goal
Try something money making for first coop job instead of doing full stack or data engineering
Take it more seriously. Still worked out well.

responses: Profs are very warm and friendly (except paul ward and oleg)
How much the time flies by
How hard co-op hunt will be
Competition can be high and it can get toxic
Everything we learned in ECE222 and ECE350
Lack of courses in desired subjects
PD, Overload 2 of them in a single work term so you don't need to any in upper years

How broad the range of topics studied was
The camaraderie between fellow EE's. Trauma bonding is a real thing and through each exam season I became closer with my classmates.
the amount of work and the justifiable complaining about the entire academic experience

I didn't expect to meet so many cool people, I thought I was only gonna really stick with my own group of friends but everyone was awesome. To the incoming ECE students, actively try to meet new people, and support eachother!
I knew it was gonna be hard, didn't realize it was gonna be that hard
The extreme demand both academically and employment

It's not the complexity, it's the grind, consistency and practice required. To-do's can climb up real quick, and I used to really get burnt out before finals (therefore barely studying for the exams). Pacing yourself, especially post-midterm, is crucial. I'd much rather miss/cut corners on some labs then get burnt out.
My closest friends were made through trauma bonding.

Before applying, to get a good feel of what the degree is like, carefully read the curriculum from the undergraduate calendar. I did not do that and I got bodied by some earlier physics and EE/hardware courses.
learn to find the light in the tunnel - bad things always come with good things

items.question: What's one piece of advice you’d give your first-year self?

responses: Unless you’re insane, your first co-ops are gonna suck
You are not alone, and a lot of people are going through similar struggles.
Take it easy. You’re young. Enjoy the next 5 years - you have the rest of your life to get stressed
Lock in early and build a good foundation of discipline
Try things to their max before deciding whether you like it or not, then ditch the stuff you don't like quickly.
Learn to Integrate and don't forget how to
Have confidence, you were accepted for a reason.
Don't follow others, they are usually wrong
Take some time to consider how important grades are vs personal enjoyment
Invest in bitcoin. More seriously though, learn to study more efficiently earlier on.
learn to not be an anti-social recluse earlier
It really gets better in fourth year

Worry/Plan less - money isn't that important. I didn't do 95% of the things I felt paranoid about I should be doing, and honestly I believe I am in the best possible situation my personal circumstances would allow me to be in. Even if I did all that I felt I should have been, I would have made slightly more money in the best case scenario

When you believe in your ability to do something, you’ll naturally put in the work to achieve it. But if you don’t believe in yourself, you’ll give up, thinking you can’t do things you’re actually capable of.
Enjoy the time, you're definitely going to miss it when its over.
Co-ops are meant to guide your future career, use them to find your technical interests.
perseverance - keep swimming and you will eventually reach the goal (dont feel rushed)
Don't Give up and study early
Pay attention in all your courses. You never know when you'll need to know something
Give less shit about how other people see you and just yap
Focus more on getting better co-ops
Time management and do an extra-curricular that isn’t stem related
Online uni is still a uni.
Work hard, apply to jobs early, bet on yourself
You have to put in more effort. Just because you made it to a big name school doesn't mean everything is guaranteed after that.
It’s okay to fail. It doesn’t matter how long it takes for you to finish ur degree. No one cares how long u took once u graduate
Seek mental health help earlier (therapy + antidepressants)
Be a bit more outgoing, msg first
Explore outside of your friend group’s activities
Network more and foster more relationships
It always works out in the end
Don’t worry about software, stick to hardware
Chatgpt will save your life, don’t stress too hard
don't panic, everything will work out
You'll pass those hard useless courses
learn software development (C or fullstack) on your own, because this degree does not teach you how to code
Enjoy the grind, have fun it'll be fine
Go to class if you’re slow learner. No point comparing yourself to others because they have different ways of doing stuff. Trust yourself and push yourself with your own skills.
